abstract: Can a large language model (LLM) improve at code generation using only its own raw outputs, without a verifier, a teacher model, or reinforcement learning? We answer in the affirmative with simple self-distillation (SSD): sample solutions from the model with certain temperature and truncation configurations, then fine-tune on those samples with standard supervised fine-tuning. SSD improves Qwen3-30B-Instruct from 42.4% to 55.3% pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6, with gains concentrating on harder problems, and it generalizes across Qwen and Llama models at 4B, 8B, and 30B scale, including both instruct and thinking variants. To understand why such a simple method can work, we trace these gains to a precision-exploration conflict in LLM decoding and show that SSD reshapes token distributions in a context-dependent way, suppressing distractor tails where precision matters while preserving useful diversity where exploration matters. Taken together, SSD offers a complementary post-training direction for improving LLM code generation.

citations.fullCitation: Geoffrey E. Hinton, Oriol Vinyals, and Jeffrey Dean. Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network. CoRR, abs/1503.02531, 2015.
citations.justification: This is the seminal paper that introduced the concept of knowledge distillation. The main paper's method, simple self-distillation (SSD), is a form of distillation that learns from the model's own outputs, and the authors explicitly contrast their teacher-free approach with the classic paradigm established by Hinton et al.
citations.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

citations.fullCitation: Yujia Li, David H. Choi, Junyoung Chung, Nate Kushman, Julian Schrittwieser, Rémi Leblond, Tom Eccles, James Keeling, Felix Gimeno, Agustin Dal Lago, Thomas Hubert, Peter Choy, Cyprien de Masson d’Autume, Igor Babuschkin, Xinyun Chen, Po-Sen Huang, Johannes Welbl, Sven Gowal, Alexey Cherepanov, James Molloy, Daniel J. Mankowitz, Esme Sutherland Robson, Pushmeet Kohli, Nando de Freitas, Koray Kavukcuoglu, and Oriol Vinyals. Competition-Level Code Generation with AlphaCode. CoRR, abs/2203.07814, 2022.
citations.justification: The AlphaCode paper represents a major paradigm in synthetic data generation for code which relies on massive-scale sampling followed by execution-based verification. The current work repeatedly emphasizes that its SSD method is simple because it requires no verifier or execution, making AlphaCode a key point of contrast for the complexity of alternative approaches.
citations.title: Competition-Level Code Generation with AlphaCode

citations.fullCitation: Eric Zelikman, Yuhuai Wu, Jesse Mu, and Noah D. Goodman. STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning. In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35: Annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems 2022, NeurIPS 2022, New Orleans, LA, USA, November 28 - December 9, 2022, 2022.
citations.justification: This paper introduces a key self-improvement method where a model is fine-tuned on its own correctly-solved problems. The current work explicitly contrasts SSD with STaR to highlight a critical difference: SSD trains directly on raw, unverified model outputs, whereas STaR requires a correctness filter, demonstrating the novelty and simplicity of the SSD approach.
citations.title: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

citations.fullCitation: Eric J. Bigelow, Ari Holtzman, Hidenori Tanaka, and Tomer D. Ullman. Forking Paths in Neural Text Generation. In The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations, ICLR 2025, 2025.
citations.justification: This citation provides the core terminology for the paper's mechanistic explanation of why SSD works. The authors' central hypothesis, the 'precision-exploration conflict,' is built on the distinction between 'locks' and 'forks' in generation, explicitly citing this paper for the concept of a 'fork,' making it fundamental to their analysis.
citations.title: Forking Paths in Neural Text Generation

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper titled "Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation."

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research paper are Ruixiang Zhang, Richard He Bai, Huangjie Zheng, Navdeep Jaitly, Ronan Collobert, and Yizhe Zhang. All authors are affiliated with Apple. Richard He Bai, Huangjie Zheng, Ruixiang Zhang, and Yizhe Zhang are noted as having contributed equally to this work.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The work addresses a challenge in the development and application of large language models (LLMs) for code generation: the increasing difficulty and cost associated with obtaining high-quality supervised training signals. Traditional methods for improving LLM code generation often rely on several external or complex components:
*   **Human-written solutions:** These are resource-intensive to produce and scale.
*   **Synthetic data pipelines:** These typically necessitate either a stronger "teacher" model for knowledge distillation (e.g., Hinton et al., 2015; Kim and Rush, 2016) or an execution-based verification environment to filter for correct solutions (e.g., Li et al., 2022; Le et al., 2022).
*   **Teacher-based distillation:** This approach inherits the performance ceiling of the teacher model, limiting potential gains.
*   **Reinforcement Learning (RL) with verifiable reward:** While powerful, RL-based methods for reasoning and coding (e.g., He et al., 2026; Shao et al., 2024) are often operationally complex and can exhibit instability.
*   **Unsupervised alternatives:** Methods using intrinsic rewards like majority voting or entropy minimization (e.g., Zuo et al., 2025; Agarwal et al., 2025) have shown early promise but have encountered issues such as reward hacking and performance collapse under extended training.

This research introduces Simple Self-Distillation (SSD), a method that aims to circumvent these dependencies by enabling an LLM to improve its code generation capabilities using *only its own raw, unverified outputs*. This approach stands in contrast to existing paradigms that require external labeled data, a separate teacher model, a verifier, or complex RL mechanisms. By operating without these external signals, SSD positions itself as a complementary post-training direction, potentially offering a more resource-efficient and scalable path to enhancing LLM performance in code generation tasks. The paper explicitly differentiates SSD from existing self-training and distillation techniques by highlighting its independence from dense signals, teachers, verifiers, and privileged information.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to investigate whether a large language model can enhance its code generation performance by training solely on solutions it generates itself, without any external verification, teacher model, or reinforcement learning. The authors explicitly pose this as a core question and aim to provide an affirmative answer through the proposed Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) method.

The detailed motivations for pursuing this objective are multi-faceted:

*   **Addressing the "High-Quality Signal" Bottleneck:** As LLMs are applied to increasingly complex coding tasks, the availability of high-quality supervised training data becomes a significant limitation. Human-written solutions are expensive, and synthetic data generation often requires either a superior teacher model or resource-intensive execution-based verification.
*   **Limitations of Current Self-Improvement Paradigms:**
    *   **Teacher-based distillation:** This approach is inherently capped by the capabilities of the teacher model.
    *   **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** While effective, RL methods for code generation can be operationally complex, prone to instability, and require carefully designed reward models or verification environments.
    *   **Unsupervised methods (intrinsic rewards):** These approaches, despite showing initial promise, have faced challenges such as reward hacking and potential performance degradation over extended training periods.
*   **Seeking Simplicity and Independence:** The authors aim to develop an "embarrassingly simple" self-improvement technique. This method should only require a set of problem prompts and the base model itself, deliberately excluding human-labeled solutions, reference answers, separate teacher models, reward models, verifiers, execution environments, or any form of reinforcement learning. The goal is to determine if a model can meaningfully improve its own performance under these minimal conditions.

A secondary, yet crucial, objective is to provide a mechanistic understanding of *why* such a simple self-distillation approach is effective. The authors hypothesize that the underlying reason lies in what they term the "precision-exploration conflict" inherent in LLM decoding for code generation:
*   **Precision-Bound "Locks":** In code, certain positions (e.g., syntax-constrained points) demand high precision, where the correct token is sharply dominant, and alternatives are primarily distractors. Here, low decoding temperatures are beneficial.
*   **Exploration-Bound "Forks":** Other positions (e.g., algorithmic choices, structural decisions) present multiple genuinely plausible continuations, requiring exploration and diversity to find optimal paths. Here, higher decoding temperatures are beneficial.
*   **The Conflict:** A single global decoding temperature setting creates a dilemma. Lowering the temperature improves precision at "locks" but suppresses diversity at "forks." Conversely, raising the temperature enables exploration at "forks" but reintroduces distractors at "locks." Any global setting is thus a compromise.

The research proposes that SSD alleviates this conflict by context-dependently reshaping the model's token distributions: aggressively suppressing distractor tails at "locks" while preserving or enhancing useful diversity at "forks." This enables a subsequent evaluation-time decoding strategy to simultaneously achieve both precision and exploration more effectively.

The contributions are summarized as:
1.  Demonstrating that SSD significantly improves code generation model performance using only the model's unverified outputs, without external teachers, verifiers, reward models, or labeled solutions.
2.  Identifying the "precision-exploration conflict" as the key underlying mechanism for SSD's effectiveness.
3.  Supporting this mechanism with empirical evidence from controlled simulations, real-model analyses, and theoretical derivations.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology of this research involved implementing Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) and conducting extensive experiments to evaluate its effectiveness across various LLMs, followed by detailed analyses to understand its underlying mechanisms.

**4.1. Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) Implementation**

The SSD process consists of three main stages:

*   **Data Synthesis:**
    *   **Prompts:** The seed subset of the rSTARcoder dataset was used, providing approximately 10,168 unique competitive programming problems after deduplication. This dataset served as the source of prompts for which the base model would generate solutions.
    *   **Sampling:** For each prompt, the *frozen pre-trained LLM* (referred to as $p_{\theta}$) generated a single candidate solution ($N=1$). This sampling was performed using specific "training-time" decoding configurations ($T_{\text{train}}$, $\rho_{\text{train}}$), which included temperature (T), top-k, and top-p truncation settings (Table 3).
    *   **No Verification:** A critical aspect of SSD is that these generated solutions were not verified for correctness. No code execution, test cases, or human filtering was applied. Only minimal syntactic filtering (e.g., removing empty responses) was performed. The raw, unverified outputs constituted the $D_{\text{SSD}}$ dataset.
    *   **Models:** Five diverse models were used as base models for self-distillation: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-4B-Instruct, Qwen3-4B-Thinking, Qwen3-30B-Instruct, and Qwen3-30B-Thinking. These spanned two model families (Llama, Qwen), three scales (4B-30B), and two reasoning styles (instruct, thinking).
    *   **Generation Environment:** vLLM (v0.11.0) was used for generation, with a 128K maximum sequence length.

*   **Training:**
    *   **Fine-tuning:** The base model was fine-tuned on the self-generated $D_{\text{SSD}}$ dataset using standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with a cross-entropy loss function ($L(\theta) = -E_{(x,y) \sim D_{\text{SSD}}} \sum_{t=1}^{|y|} \log p_{\theta}(y_t | x, y_{<t})$).
    *   **Infrastructure & Hyperparameters:** Training utilized Megatron-LM on 8x B200 GPUs. Optimization was performed with AdamW (cosine decay, peak learning rate $5 \times 10^{-6}$, global batch size 32, sequence length 65,536). Instruct models were trained for 2,500 iterations, and thinking models for 300 iterations.

*   **Inference:**
    *   **Deployment:** The fine-tuned model ($p_{\theta}^*$) was deployed for evaluation.
    *   **Decoding Configuration:** Inference employed specific "evaluation-time" decoding settings ($T_{\text{eval}}$, $\rho_{\text{eval}}$), including temperature, top-k, and top-p (Table 3). These settings were distinct from those used for the base model's official recommendations (Table 4).

**4.2. Evaluation**

*   **Benchmarks:**
    *   Primary: LiveCodeBench v6 (LCB v6), containing 131 problems stratified by difficulty (easy, medium, hard).
    *   Secondary: LiveCodeBench v5, a larger set of 374 problems.
*   **Metrics:** Pass@1 and Pass@5 were reported, estimated using 10 independent samples per problem.
*   **Out-of-Domain Transfer:** To assess whether SSD specialized the models too narrowly, evaluation was conducted on benchmarks for math reasoning (AIME), general code generation (HumanEval Python/Shell), code understanding (CruxEval), and general knowledge (MMLU).

**4.3. Investigation into "Why SSD Works"**

The authors pursued a three-pronged approach to validate their "precision-exploration conflict" hypothesis:

1.  **Controlled Toy Simulation:**
    *   A minimal finite-state machine (FSM) was designed with a 16-token vocabulary.
    *   The FSM included distinct state archetypes: a "root" state, "fork" states (where multiple continuations are plausible), and "lock" states (where one token is highly dominant but a distractor tail exists).
    *   Successful trajectories required passing through a fork and multiple lock states.
    *   The environment allowed for exact computation of success probabilities under various decoding temperatures and truncation settings, enabling a precise comparison of base vs. SSD model behavior.

2.  **Real-Model Analysis:**
    *   Compared token distributions of the base Qwen3-30B-Instruct model with its SSD-trained counterpart on LCB v6.
    *   Analyzed cumulative probability mass across token ranks to assess the "cleanliness" of the head and the weakness of the distractor tail.
    *   Examined the number of surviving tokens and the entropy of the distribution after truncation across different evaluation temperatures ($T_{\text{eval}}$) to understand how SSD affects exploration.

3.  **Theoretical Decomposition:**
    *   Formalized the SSD training objective, showing how sampling with $T_{\text{train}}$ and $\rho_{\text{train}}$ induces a modified target distribution.
    *   Decomposed the cross-entropy loss into components related to "support compression" (driven by truncation) and "within-support reshaping" (driven by training temperature).
    *   Explained how these components lead to asymmetric changes at "lock" vs. "fork" contexts and how total entropy reduction can coexist with increased useful head entropy for exploration.
    *   Provided a theoretical basis for why decode-only tuning on the base model cannot replicate SSD's gains, citing "prefix rigidity" and "power rigidity" constraints.

**4.4. Hyperparameter Interaction Studies**

A grid search was performed with Qwen3-30B-Instruct on LCB v6 to analyze the interaction between $T_{\text{train}}$ and $T_{\text{eval}}$, both with and without training-time truncation (top-k and top-p). This aimed to identify optimal settings and understand how temperature and truncation compose to influence performance.

**4.5. High-Temperature Stress Test ("Bad Data, Good Results")**

A specific experiment was conducted where Qwen3-30B-Instruct was trained using self-generated data synthesized with a very high $T_{\text{train}}=2.0$ and *no truncation* (vacuous $\rho_{\text{train}}$). This intentionally produced largely incoherent or "gibberish" training data. The objective was to assess if SSD's benefits persisted even when the quality of individual samples was low, thereby isolating the contribution of distributional reshaping from reliance on inherently good samples.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research yielded several findings regarding the efficacy and mechanisms of Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) for code generation.

**5.1. Substantial Performance Improvement in Code Generation**
*   SSD consistently improved pass@1 scores across all five evaluated models from the Llama and Qwen families (4B-30B parameters, instruct and thinking variants).
*   For Qwen3-30B-Instruct, pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6 (LCB v6) increased from 42.4% to 55.3%, representing a 12.9 percentage point (pp) absolute gain and a 30.4% relative improvement.
*   Other models showed gains: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (+3.5pp), Qwen3-4B-Instruct (+7.5pp), Qwen3-4B-Thinking (+3.3pp), and Qwen3-30B-Thinking (+2.1pp).
*   Similar improvements were observed on the larger LCB v5 benchmark, with Qwen3-30B-Instruct improving from 45.8% to 54.3% pass@1 (+8.5pp).

**5.2. Gains Concentrated on More Difficult Problems**
*   The performance improvements were not uniform across problem difficulties. For Qwen3-30B-Instruct on LCB v6, pass@1 gains were +6.5pp on easy problems, +14.2pp on medium problems, and +15.3pp on hard problems.
*   Pass@5 gains exhibited an even greater concentration on harder problems: +6.6pp (easy), +19.6pp (medium), and +23.0pp (hard). This pattern was consistent across other Qwen models as well.

**5.3. Preservation and Improvement of Diversity**
*   A notable pattern was that pass@5 gains were often larger than corresponding pass@1 gains (e.g., +18.1pp vs. +12.9pp for Qwen3-30B-Instruct overall on LCB v6; +23.0pp vs. +15.3pp on hard problems).
*   This observation suggests that SSD does not merely sharpen a single dominant solution path but also preserves or enhances the diversity of viable solutions across generation samples, contributing to broader coverage improvements.

**5.4. Superiority over Global Decoding Policy Tuning**
*   Extensive sweeps of evaluation-time decoding parameters (temperature and truncation) on the *base model* demonstrated only modest performance variations (e.g., a 2.2pp spread for Qwen3-30B-Instruct pass@1).
*   SSD consistently outperformed the best-tuned base model configuration by substantial margins (e.g., +11.8pp for Qwen3-30B-Instruct pass@1, and even larger margins on hard problems and at pass@5).
*   This finding indicates that SSD induces fundamental changes within the model's learned distributions that cannot be replicated by merely adjusting inference-time decoding parameters on the original model.

**5.5. Hyperparameter Interaction and Effective Temperature**
*   In experiments without truncation during data synthesis, training-time temperature ($T_{\text{train}}$) and evaluation-time temperature ($T_{\text{eval}}$) composed multiplicatively, with performance largely organized by an "effective temperature" ($T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{train}} \cdot T_{\text{eval}}$). A quadratic peak was observed near $T_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.2$.
*   When training-time truncation was applied, the overall performance ceiling increased, suggesting that truncation provides an additional mechanism for improvement beyond simple temperature composition. The optimal $T_{\text{eff}}$ also tended to shift towards higher values with more stringent truncation.

**5.6. Mechanistic Explanation: Alleviating the Precision-Exploration Conflict**
*   **Toy Simulation Validation:** The controlled FSM environment confirmed the "precision-exploration conflict." The base model exhibited a compromise in decoding temperature. After simulated SSD, locks became more robust (less sensitive to temperature changes), while forks maintained useful diversity. This enabled the student model to achieve higher success probabilities at significantly higher optimal decoding temperatures.
*   **Real-Model Evidence:**
    *   Analysis of Qwen3-30B-Instruct showed that SSD led to a faster accumulation of cumulative probability mass at the top ranks, indicating a "cleaner head" and weaker diffuse distractor tails (consistent with "lock" behavior).
    *   The SSD model exhibited a stronger increase in entropy and number of surviving tokens with rising $T_{\text{eval}}$ compared to the base model. This suggested that SSD makes $T_{\text{eval}}$ more effective at exploring viable alternatives near the head ("fork" behavior).
*   **Theoretical Framework:** The SSD objective was decomposed into "support compression" (via truncation, pushing mass onto a smaller, cleaner set of tokens) and "within-support reshaping" (via training temperature, smoothing probabilities within the retained set). This framework explains why locks become sharper (support compression dominates) and forks retain explorable diversity (reshaping acts within the clean head). It also clarifies how total entropy can decrease (due to tail suppression) while useful conditional head entropy is preserved or increased.
*   **Limitation of Decode-Only Tuning:** Theoretical analysis indicated that decode-only policies are constrained by "prefix rigidity" (requiring all higher-ranked tokens to be included) and "power rigidity" (scaling all log-odds within the retained support by a single factor). SSD, by changing the underlying token distributions, circumvents these rigidities, allowing for context-dependent transformations that a fixed decoding rule cannot achieve.

**5.7. Robustness to Degraded Training Data ("Bad Data, Good Results")**
*   In a stress test where Qwen3-30B-Instruct was trained on self-generated data synthesized with a high $T_{\text{train}}=2.0$ and no truncation (resulting in largely incoherent/gibberish samples), the fine-tuned model still achieved significant improvements.
*   Pass@1 rose to 48.1% (+5.7pp) and pass@5 to 64.0% (+10.5pp), with gains concentrated on hard problems.
*   This finding suggests that the core benefit of SSD stems more from the distributional reshaping mechanisms (support compression and within-support flattening) than from the superficial correctness of the individual generated training samples. The improvements in this regime still depended on evaluation-time truncation to clean up diffuse tails not addressed during training.

**5.8. Out-of-Domain Transfer Performance**
*   For the 30B models, performance on math reasoning (AIME), general code generation (HumanEval), code understanding (CruxEval), and general knowledge (MMLU) remained broadly stable, with changes generally within ±2 percentage points.
*   Smaller models exhibited more varied, benchmark-dependent tradeoffs, with some metrics improving and others showing regressions (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct saw AIME scores drop but HumanEval and CruxEval improve).

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research presents a Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) method that allows large language models to improve their code generation capabilities using only their own raw, unverified outputs. This work carries several implications for the field of LLM development and application:

*   **Resource Efficiency and Scalability:** SSD significantly reduces the reliance on expensive external resources such as human-labeled datasets, sophisticated teacher models, or computationally intensive code execution environments for verification. This makes the process of improving LLM code generation more accessible and scalable, potentially democratizing advanced model refinement.
*   **Unlocking Latent Model Capabilities:** The findings suggest that current LLMs may possess latent capabilities for code generation that are not fully realized under standard, fixed decoding policies. SSD offers a method to unlock this potential without requiring complex architectural changes or novel training paradigms.
*   **Deeper Understanding of LLM Learning:** The identification and detailed explanation of the "precision-exploration conflict" and how SSD alleviates it provides a mechanistic understanding of how self-distillation reshapes token distributions. This contributes to the theoretical framework of LLM behavior, particularly in structured generation tasks like coding, by demonstrating how a global training objective can induce context-dependent changes in local distributions.
*   **Complementary Post-Training Approach:** SSD offers a distinct and complementary direction for post-training refinement, positioning itself alongside methods like instruction tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), or knowledge distillation. Its simplicity and independence from external signals could make it an attractive initial or supplementary step in model development pipelines.
*   **Robustness to Data Quality:** The "bad data, good results" stress test highlights that the core benefits of SSD stem from distributional reshaping rather than the superficial quality or correctness of individual self-generated samples. This suggests a resilience that could be valuable in scenarios where high-quality synthetic data is difficult to produce, broadening the applicability of self-improvement techniques.
*   **Implications for Decoding Strategies:** The work underscores the limitations of solely tuning inference-time decoding parameters. By demonstrating that SSD fundamentally alters the model's underlying probability distributions, it suggests that future research might explore dynamic or context-aware decoding strategies built upon such reshaped models.

In summary, SSD provides a method for self-improvement in LLM code generation that is both effective and minimally resource-dependent. Its demonstrated ability to enhance performance, particularly on challenging problems, while being grounded in a clear mechanistic explanation, positions it as a relevant advancement in the field of large language model development.
overview:
## Simple Self-Distillation for Code Generation

Large language models (LLMs) have become remarkably proficient at generating computer code, but continuing to improve them often hits a resource bottleneck. Traditional methods for enhancing these models typically require high-quality supervised signals, such as human-written solutions, a more powerful "teacher" model for knowledge distillation, or complex reinforcement learning (RL) loops that rely on code execution and automated test cases.

![Figure 1: Overview of Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) results and comparative advantages over existing methods.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01193v1/x1.png)

A recent research effort introduces a method called Simple Self-Distillation (SSD), which attempts to bypass these dependencies entirely. The core premise is that an LLM can improve its code generation capabilities by training solely on its own unverified outputs. Unlike existing self-improvement techniques, SSD does not use any external verification (like checking if the code actually runs correctly), nor does it use a teacher model to provide labels. This "embarrassingly simple" approach aims to determine if a model can refine its own internal logic and token distributions through a basic loop of generation and fine-tuning.

## The Core Concept: Avoiding External Signals

The significance of SSD lies in its independence. Most current state-of-the-art code models are trained using one of the following:
*   **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Requires human-written or teacher-generated reference solutions.
*   **Execution-Based Filtering:** Generates many solutions and keeps only those that pass unit tests (e.g., CodeContests or AlphaCode).
*   **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Uses a reward model or an execution environment to provide feedback on generated code.

![Figure 2: Comparing SSD with other training paradigms in terms of signal density and external dependencies.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01193v1/x2.png)

SSD, by contrast, operates under a much stricter constraint. It takes a pre-trained model and a set of coding problem prompts. The model generates solutions for these prompts, and then the same model is fine-tuned on these self-generated responses. The researchers found that even without knowing which solutions are correct, this process consistently leads to better performance on standardized coding benchmarks.

## The SSD Methodology

The SSD process follows a straightforward three-stage pipeline:

1.  **Data Synthesis:** A frozen, pre-trained LLM ($p_{\theta}$) is given a set of coding prompts. For every prompt, the model generates exactly one candidate solution ($N=1$). This generation uses specific training-time decoding settings ($T_{\text{train}}$, $\rho_{\text{train}}$), which include temperature and truncation (like top-k or top-p). Crucially, these solutions are not verified for correctness; the model simply produces its best guess or a diverse sample.
2.  **Fine-Tuning:** The raw, unverified outputs are collected into a dataset ($D_{\text{SSD}}$). The base model is then fine-tuned on this dataset using standard supervised fine-tuning. The training objective is to minimize the cross-entropy loss:
$$L(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim D_{\text{SSD}}} \sum_{t=1}^{|y|} \log p_{\theta}(y_t | x, y_{<t})$$
Essentially, the model is being taught to prefer the types of distributions it generated during the synthesis stage.
3.  **Inference:** The resulting fine-tuned model ($p_{\theta}^*$) is then evaluated using different evaluation-time decoding settings ($T_{\text{eval}}$, $\rho_{\text{eval}}$).

The researchers tested this on several model families, including Llama-3.1 and Qwen, across various sizes (4B to 30B parameters). Across the board, models showed significant gains in pass@1 and pass@5 metrics, especially on difficult problems. For example, Qwen3-30B-Instruct saw its pass@1 score on LiveCodeBench v6 jump from 42.4% to 55.3%, a 12.9 percentage point increase.

## The Mechanism: The Precision-Exploration Conflict

A major contribution of this work is the explanation for *why* such a simple method works. The authors hypothesize that LLMs face a "precision-exploration conflict" when decoding code.

In coding, certain parts of a file require absolute precision—for example, a colon at the end of an `if` statement or the correct syntax for a library call. These are "Locks." At these positions, the model needs to be very sharp, and low temperatures are helpful to avoid distractors. However, other parts of the code represent algorithmic choices or structural decisions where multiple valid paths exist. These are "Forks." Here, the model needs to explore different possibilities to find a successful path, which usually requires higher temperatures.

![Figure 3: Illustration of the "Fork" (exploration-bound) and "Lock" (precision-bound) contexts in code generation.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01193v1/x7.png)

A single global temperature setting is always a compromise:
*   **Low temperature:** Sharpens "Locks" (good) but kills diversity at "Forks" (bad).
*   **High temperature:** Allows exploration at "Forks" (good) but introduces errors at "Locks" by raising the probability of distractor tokens (bad).

SSD reshapes the model's internal distributions to alleviate this conflict. By training on truncated samples at a specific temperature, the model learns to suppress the "tail" of the distribution (the unlikely distractors) while preserving the diversity of the "head" (the plausible alternatives). This allows the model to maintain precision at "Locks" even when a high temperature is used during evaluation to encourage exploration at "Forks."

## Robustness and the "Gibberish" Experiment

One of the most striking findings of the paper is the "Bad Data, Good Results" experiment. The researchers intentionally generated training data using a very high temperature ($T_{\text{train}}=2.0$) and no truncation. This resulted in "gibberish" training data—responses that were largely incoherent or garbled.

![Figure 4: Results of training on "gibberish" data synthesized at high temperature, showing performance gains despite low data quality.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01193v1/x10.png)

Despite the poor quality of individual samples, fine-tuning on this data still led to significant performance improvements over the base model. This suggests that the benefits of SSD are not necessarily derived from the "correctness" of the individual solutions in the training set. Instead, the benefit comes from the *distributional reshaping*—the mathematical process of how the fine-tuning objective changes the model's probability mass across different ranks of tokens.

## Detailed Results and Trends

The performance improvements were particularly pronounced on harder problems. In benchmarks like LiveCodeBench, problems are categorized by difficulty: easy, medium, and hard. For the Qwen3-30B-Instruct model, while "easy" problems saw a 6.5 percentage point gain, "hard" problems saw a 15.3 percentage point gain.

![Figure 5: Detailed performance table showing pass@1 and pass@5 gains across different difficulty levels and model sizes.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.01193v1/x3.png)

Furthermore, SSD significantly improved the "pass@5" score—a metric that measures if any of five independent samples for a problem is correct. This indicates that SSD is not just making the model more confident in its first guess, but is also improving the overall quality and diversity of its possible solutions.

The researchers also conducted a grid search to understand the interaction between training temperature ($T_{\text{train}}$) and evaluation temperature ($T_{\text{eval}}$). They found that performance is often governed by an "effective temperature" ($T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{train}} \cdot T_{\text{eval}}$). Without truncation, performance peaked around $T_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.2$. However, adding truncation (like top-k) during data synthesis pushed the performance ceiling even higher, showing that removing distractors from the training data is a key component of SSD's success.

## Theoretical Foundations

To validate the "precision-exploration conflict" theory, the authors used a controlled toy simulation using a Finite-State Machine (FSM). They created a world with distinct "fork" states and "lock" states and mathematically calculated success probabilities. The simulation confirmed that a base model is always stuck in a compromise, while a model trained via SSD can achieve higher success rates by allowing for higher evaluation temperatures without failing at the "locks."

Theoretical decomposition of the cross-entropy loss also showed that SSD performs two main actions:
1.  **Support Compression:** It pushes probability mass away from low-ranked distractor tokens toward a smaller set of high-ranked tokens.
2.  **Within-Support Reshaping:** It smooths the probabilities among the high-ranked tokens, preserving their relative diversity.

This dual action explains why the model becomes more robust. It clears the "noise" (the distractors) but keeps the "signal" (the viable alternatives).

## Significance and Future Impact

The introduction of Simple Self-Distillation represents a shift toward more resource-efficient model improvement. By proving that models can improve by simply "talking to themselves" and refining their internal distributions, the research opens several doors:

*   **Democratic Model Refinement:** Smaller organizations that lack massive clusters for RL or the resources for massive human-labeling campaigns can potentially use SSD to squeeze more performance out of existing open-weights models.
*   **Scale and Scope:** SSD can be applied to any prompt set, regardless of whether a ground-truth solution exists. This makes it highly scalable for niche domains where labeled data is scarce.
*   **Complementary Training:** SSD is not a replacement for RL or teacher-based distillation; instead, it is a complementary post-training step. It can be used as a final "polishing" phase to optimize how a model uses its learned knowledge during inference.

In conclusion, SSD demonstrates that the way a model chooses its next token is as important as the information it contains. By alleviating the fundamental conflict between needing precision and needing exploration, SSD allows LLMs to better navigate the complexities of code generation without requiring external guidance.

keyInsights: Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) alleviates the "precision-exploration conflict" by context-dependently reshaping the model's token distributions, suppressing distractors at "locks" while maintaining useful diversity at "forks."
SSD induces fundamental changes within the model's learned distributions that cannot be replicated by merely adjusting inference-time decoding parameters on the original model.

The method's benefits stem from distributional reshaping (support compression and within-support smoothing) rather than the superficial correctness of individual self-generated training samples, as improvements persist even with intentionally degraded training data.
originalProblem: High cost and difficulty of obtaining high-quality supervised training signals for LLM code generation, limiting scalability.

Limitations of existing self-improvement methods, including performance ceilings for teacher-based distillation and operational complexities or instabilities in RL and unsupervised intrinsic reward systems.

An inherent "precision-exploration conflict" in LLM decoding for code, where a single global temperature setting struggles to balance precise token generation at constrained points with necessary exploration for diverse algorithmic choices.
results: SSD consistently increased pass@1 scores across five evaluated models, with Qwen3-30B-Instruct improving by 12.9 percentage points (from 42.4% to 55.3%) on LiveCodeBench v6.
Performance gains were disproportionately higher for more challenging problems, showing pass@1 gains of up to 15.3pp and pass@5 gains of 23.0pp on hard problems for Qwen3-30B-Instruct.

The SSD-trained model substantially outperformed the best-tuned base model configuration by 11.8pp for Qwen3-30B-Instruct pass@1, indicating that SSD's changes are not achievable through decoding parameter adjustments alone.
solution: Introduce Simple Self-Distillation (SSD), where a frozen base LLM generates a single candidate solution for each problem prompt using specific training-time decoding configurations, without any external verification.
Fine-tune the base LLM on this self-generated, unverified dataset using standard supervised fine-tuning with a cross-entropy loss function.
Deploy the resulting fine-tuned model with optimized evaluation-time decoding settings, designed to leverage the reshaped token distributions for improved performance.
summary.summary: A method called Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) enables large language models to enhance their code generation performance by training exclusively on self-generated, unverified solutions. This approach improved the Qwen3-30B-Instruct model's pass@1 score on LiveCodeBench v6 from 42.4% to 55.3%, demonstrating its efficacy without external supervision or complex verification.

title: Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation
